I watch my colleagues exchange nervous glances and forced smiles; they're like actors on a stage playing out their roles in this corporate tragedy. Some try to hide disappointment, others attempt humor as if it will make the situation any less painful - but nothing works here.

As I listen more closely, Mr. Thompson continues his passive-aggressive monologue about 'unnecessary expenses' and how we must prioritize our resources like some sort of financial triage team in a war zone (which is not quite accurate since there are no actual wars going on).

I can feel my frustration bubbling up inside me, but I know better than to let it show. After all, who wants to be seen as the office troublemaker? Not that anyone cares about being liked in this place - they just want their paychecks and a semblance of job security.

My mind races as I try to process the implications - fewer ad campaigns mean less exposure for potential clients and a smaller team means more workload for those who remain.

As Mr. Thompson wraps up his speech with a dismissive wave of his hand (as though he's done nothing more than casually mention these changes), there is an awkward silence in the room that seems almost palpable - like everyone holding their breath waiting for someone else to break it.

But no one does. Instead, we all fall into our respective roles as if this were just another day at work: nodding along with forced smiles and polite congratulations on 'efficient budgeting.' I can't help but wonder how long these cutbacks will last before they start affecting us directly.

As the meeting ends, my colleagues begin to file out of the room one by one. Some exchange hushed words about their concerns while others try not to let on that something is amiss - after all, we're professionals here and should be able to handle this without showing any weakness.

I stand there for a moment longer than necessary before I too begin my walk back to the office. My mind continues to race with thoughts about what lies ahead: Will our clients suffer because of these cutbacks? How will we manage when things get even tougher down the line?
